title: Consumer Theory & Choice
description: Utility, scarcity, opportunity cost, consumer equilibrium, and the law of diminishing marginal utility

questions.statement: Utility means the objective usefulness of a good.
questions.payload: FALSE. Utility is subjective satisfaction, not objective usefulness.

questions.statement: A cigarette has utility for a smoker even though it is harmful.
questions.payload: TRUE. Utility depends on personal preference, not health or usefulness.

questions.statement: Total utility reaches its maximum when marginal utility becomes zero.
questions.payload: TRUE. When MU = 0, consuming more adds no satisfaction and TU peaks.

questions.statement: Total utility falls whenever marginal utility is falling.
questions.payload: FALSE. TU still rises as long as MU is positive, even when MU is falling.

questions.statement: Marginal utility can become negative.
questions.payload: TRUE. Beyond a certain point, additional units reduce total satisfaction.

questions.statement: Cardinal utility assumes satisfaction can be measured in exact numbers called utils.
questions.payload: TRUE. Cardinal utility treats satisfaction as quantifiable in precise units.

questions.statement: Ordinal utility requires that we measure satisfaction in exact numerical units.
questions.payload: FALSE. Ordinal utility only ranks preferences as first, second, or third.

questions.statement: Both cardinal and ordinal utility assume the consumer is rational.
questions.payload: TRUE. Both approaches rely on the assumption that consumers make consistent choices.

questions.statement: Scarcity and shortage mean exactly the same thing in economics.
questions.payload: FALSE. Scarcity is permanent and universal, while shortage is temporary and specific.

questions.statement: Opportunity cost is the money spent on the chosen good.
questions.payload: FALSE. Opportunity cost is the value of the next-best alternative forgone, not the money spent.

questions.statement: Even wealthy countries face the problem of scarcity.
questions.payload: TRUE. Scarcity is permanent because human wants are unlimited and resources are finite.

questions.statement: A rational consumer stops consuming when marginal utility becomes negative.
questions.payload: TRUE. No rational consumer buys a unit that reduces total satisfaction.

questions.statement: At consumer equilibrium, MUx/Px equals MUy/Py for all goods purchased.
questions.payload: TRUE. A rational consumer allocates income so that MUx/Px = MUy/Py.

questions.statement: If MUx/Px > MUy/Py, the consumer should buy less of good X.
questions.payload: FALSE. The consumer should buy more of good X until the ratios are equal.

questions.statement: The law of diminishing marginal utility states that total utility decreases with each additional unit consumed.
questions.payload: FALSE. The law states that marginal utility decreases, not total utility.

questions.statement: A consumer with a budget of 10 birr can be in equilibrium while spending only 8 birr.
questions.payload: FALSE. Consumer equilibrium requires the entire budget to be spent.

questions.statement: Cardinal utility theory assumes diminishing marginal utility.
questions.payload: TRUE. Cardinal utility explicitly assumes that each additional unit adds less satisfaction.

questions.statement: Ordinal utility theory does not assume diminishing marginal utility.
questions.payload: TRUE. Ordinal utility relies on ranking preferences, not on diminishing marginal utility.

questions.statement: The first cup of coffee in an Ethiopian coffee ceremony gives the highest marginal utility.
questions.payload: TRUE. The law of diminishing marginal utility means the first unit typically yields the greatest extra satisfaction.

questions.statement: Shortage is a permanent condition caused by unlimited wants and limited resources.
questions.payload: FALSE. Shortage is temporary and market-specific; scarcity is the permanent condition.
